narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 36, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, and we appreciate your interest in our law school. Your background includes lower-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which aligns with the majority of applicants who pursue law school that way. However, the admissions model we use to predict first-time bar exam success has assessed your overall profile and estimated a 58.7% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—above the threshold we set for admission, leading to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students. The two biggest factors pushing the model toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably below the typical 5th or 6th decile for admitted students, signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers. Similarly, your 1st-year decile of 1st—far below the average—stood out as the second strongest influence, suggesting early academic challenges that the model associates with higher bar exam risk. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, close to the dataset average, worked strongly in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, as did your above-average undergraduate GPA. Your full-time enrollment plan also nudged things positively, matching the norm for successful bar takers. Family income had almost no impact here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and mid-undergrad deciles are in the lower tail of the distribution, which outweighed your other strengths in the model's eyes. To move forward, we recommend retaking courses or pursuing targeted prep to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas—many students like you turn things around with focused improvement. We're rooting for your success in law, and we'd be happy to review an updated application. Keep pushing forward.
